title: Disaggregated Inference: 18 Months Later
summary: This retrospective explains why prefill-decode disaggregation moved from research novelty to production pattern. It is valuable because it does not just praise disaggregation; it also explains the scheduling, utilization, and systems constraints that determine when the split is worth it.
whyRelevant: This is the best architectural starting point for the spike. It matches the team’s April planning bet that rollout inference should be disaggregated from training when the capacity model rewards specialization.

title: Disaggregated Prefill and Decode
summary: Perplexity's article turns the abstract prefill/decode split into a transport problem: the system needs to move layer state and metadata between specialized instances efficiently enough that the split still wins. The details around layer transfer and notification make the communication boundary concrete.
whyRelevant: It grounds the spike in an actual production design. Even though the article focuses on KV/state transfer rather than weight deltas, it shows the exact serving boundary the team will have to coordinate with SGLang in the Tinker workstream.

title: Composer 2 Technical Report
summary: Cursor's Composer 2 report is the freshest public example of a frontier async RL stack that makes weight synchronization an explicit systems component. The report describes per-step delta compression, sharded upload and download, background hotload signaling, and geographically distributed inference clusters reconstructing checkpoints from a shared delta chain.
whyRelevant: This is the closest new public proof point for the exact operating model the Training team wants: disaggregated rollout inference, frequent policy updates, and no requirement for direct network connectivity between training and every rollout cluster. It makes the Modal/Tinker framing feel less speculative.

title: Scaling and Optimizing Frontier Model Training
summary: Fireworks frames the Composer 2 collaboration from the platform side: model catalog, parallelism stack, deployment bring-up, and RL workflows with separate policy and reference deployments. It is useful because it ties delta-compressed sync and rollout fleets to a concrete training product rather than a one-off lab stack.
whyRelevant: For the Training team, this is the practical complement to the Cursor report. It suggests what a reusable platform surface for disaggregated RL could look like, which is much closer to the team's goal of a repeatable Modal example than a single impressive benchmark.

title: Weight Transfer for RL Post-Training in under 2 seconds
summary: This is the most concrete public writeup in the set for the RL side of disaggregation. It explains how a training cluster and an inference cluster stay synchronized using point-to-point RDMA writes, pipelined task stages, and a static transfer schedule, bringing trillion-parameter updates down to roughly 1.3 seconds.
whyRelevant: If the question is 'what actually makes the team’s disaggregated rollout inference plan plausible?', this is the clearest operational answer. It is also the closest public analogue to the Perplexity ask called out in April planning.

title: RDMA Point-to-Point Communication for LLM Systems
summary: Perplexity generalizes the transport ideas behind its serving and RL systems into TransferEngine, a point-to-point RDMA communication layer. The article is especially useful because it places weight transfer and disaggregated inference in the same design family rather than treating them as separate optimization problems.
whyRelevant: This source ties the spike together. It shows that once systems move beyond collectives, the same point-to-point substrate can serve KV transfer, MoE routing, and fast weight synchronization, which is exactly the kind of reusable foundation the team needs for Modal-native examples.

title: Understanding and Exploiting Weight Update Sparsity for Communication-Efficient Distributed RL
summary: PULSE is the strongest public paper in the set for the actual delta-compression angle. It argues that RL fine-tuning updates are extremely sparse and that lossless sparse patch encoding can reduce synchronization bandwidth by roughly two orders of magnitude without changing training dynamics.
whyRelevant: This is the source that most directly answers the spike title. It turns 'delta compression for weight updates' from a vague systems instinct into a concrete communication strategy with measured bandwidth savings, which is why it maps cleanly onto the team’s planned multinode guide work.
